content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 1, 2016

Sue Rae Irelan
California Tahoe Conservancy
1061 Third Street
South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-200

Dear Ms. Irelan:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding your duties as an Associate
Environmental Planner at the California Tahoe Conservancy (the "Conservancy") under the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May you interact with Laulima Corporation, California State Parks, and other interested
parties in relation to shared parking in the area of the Kings Beach State Recreation Area (the
"KBSRA") despite your spouse's employment by Laulima in connection with a project near the
KBSRA?

CONCLUSION

You may take part in the KBSRA decisions, as the decisions do not appear to have a
reasonably foreseeable material effect on your interests.

FACTS

You are an Associate Environmental Planner with the Conservancy and have been assigned
as the Conservancy's Project Manager for the Kings Beach Park General Plan Revision and Pier
Rebuilt Project (the "Project"). The Project is a partnership effort with California State Parks and
the Conservancy. The Project seeks to revise the State Parks' General Plan for KBSRA and
proposes to rebuild and potentially relocate the existing pier on site. The KBSRA is located in the

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No A-16-200
Page No. 2

heart of the commercial district of Kings Beach, California, a small unincorporated community on
Tahoe's North Shore. According to the State Parks website, the KBSRA features 979 feet of
frontage property along the North Shore of Lake Tahoe.

Your role in the Project is to represent Conservancy interests throughout the planning
process. You serve as a liaison between decisionmakers in your agency and the Project team. You
generally present data and discussions to your supervisors, receive their direction, and work to
implement their direction. More specifically, according to the legal memorandum attached to your
request, you work directly with State Parks staff on the development of alternatives for the Project
as the Project is viewed as a coordinated State Project. Primary approvals for the project are through
State Parks. Your responsibilities include:

• Participating in meetings with State Parks and their consultants;

• Participating in meetings with other public agencies and private parties;

• Participating in design charrettes;³

• Reviewing plans and documents;

• Providing direct input on these plans and documents that is incorporated;

• Involving or seeking input of other agency staff as required;

• Representing the Conservancy at public workshops and visioning sessions, which includes
presentations and direct facilitation of these meetings;

• Overseeing the terms of interagency and grant agreements associated with the project and
the Conservancy's financial contributions to the project;

• Informing senior management and seeking their input and concurrence on project
alternatives, agency direction, etc.;

• Obtaining necessary Conservancy Board approvals to support the agency's role, which
includes funding and land ownership, in the project.

Your husband, Gordon Shaw, is a transportation engineer and corporate officer at LSC
Transportation Consultants, Inc. Mr. Shaw is also a 10% owner of LSC. His firm provides
transportation consultant services such as traffic and parking studies, transit studies, and bicycle and
pedestrian studies, among other planning services. The firm maintains three offices and works
throughout the West. The office at which Mr. Shaw works is located in Lake Forest, near Tahoe
City, California. You stated that he often works for private and public clients throughout the Tahoe-
Truckee area.

³ Generally, a Charrette is understood to mean an intense, participative process where all members of a
community work directly with a multidisciplinary design team to define a collective plan vision. (See e.g.,
http://www.holtville.ca.gov/blueprint/?q=node 14.)

File No A-16-200
Page No. 3

LSC has agreed to work with Laulima Corporation and Brian Helm, their local planning representative, on a redevelopment project located in the City of Kings Beach (the "Crown Project"). The Crown Project is within approximately 1,000 feet of the KBSRA. You stated that Mr. Shaw has no ownership interest in Laulima and the outcome of the Crown Project will not affect the compensation Mr. Shaw expects to receive from his professional services under the contract with the developer. According to the memorandum attached to your request:

• The Crown Project proposes the redevelopment of an existing hotel and several properties in the Kings Beach area that are in close proximity to KBSRA.

• In addition to parking and transportation planning services, LSC will review environmental documentation prepared for the Crown Project and will likely review and provide comments on the environmental document. Mr. Shaw will be directly involved in these tasks. However, Mr. Shaw's firm provides only planning services and would not be involved in the development of construction drawings for the Crown Project.

Subsequent to the execution of the LSC/Laulima contract Laulima became the successful bidder for a public-private partnership to construct a new North Tahoe Event Center. The Events Center is owned by the North Tahoe Public Utility District (the "District") and is immediately adjacent to the KBSRA and currently relies on parking available at the KBSRA for people attending events at their facility. A parties recently entered into an Agreement to Negotiate Exclusively. The proposal is described in pertinent part, as follows:

• The initial term of the lease will be 30 years and can be extended.

• Laulima will invest not less than $5.6 million in project costs for the leased property.

• Laulima shall pay Lessor a base amount of $50,000 annually for the first two years; $75,000 for the third year; and $100,000 for the fourth year, plus a percentage of Lessee's gross revenue from · sales from the leasehold to be negotiated by the parties. Commencing on the 5th year, the base rent shall escalate on an annual basis based on an index to be agreed upon by the parties.

• Laulima shall make the property available for public use and events consistent with District's past operation of the facility.

• According to the District website, the building and the property will continue to be owned by the District.

On October 12, 2016, you provided the following additional information:

• You intend to leave the Conservancy as of January 23, 2017. You continue to work on the Project.

• There are currently 177 total parking spaces in the KBSRA.

File No A-16-200
Page No. 4

• The range of alternatives for parking under current consideration is: 192, 199, and 167
spots. One of the alternatives removes all the parking nearest the Events Center.
However, since KBSRA is small park, the majority of the parking on site will be within
500 feet of the Events Center in all alternatives. Increasingly, people headed to the
Events Center (and beach) choose to park along the highway or side streets outside of
the KBSRA parking lot where parking is free to avoid paying at all to park.

• Under current conditions, the Events Center is underutilized. A typical day includes a
few recreation classes with a very low parking need (5-10 vehicles). The largest events
held are community events such as July 4th or the Christmas Faire, which are often held
in the evening and probably require 100+ spaces. One of the reasons that the District
intends to come to agreement with a private developer is to better utilize the building
which will increase parking need.

• To your knowledge, there is not an agreement between State Parks and the District that
captures full compensation for parking. For example, when the Events Center is used by
the District Court to screen potential jurors, there is no parking fee charged.

• Alternatively, the District keeps track of participation in commercial uses of the building
and pays State Parks for parking from facility fees charged. During the main part of the
summer season, State Parks has a staffed kiosk at the entrance to the parking lot and
everyone who enters pays whether they are headed to the beach or the Events Center. In
the off season and in the evenings, the kiosk is not staffed and very few users follow the
sign direction for self-registration with payment.

Staff has been directed, given the recent developments at the Event Center and the Crown
Project, to engage in broader conversations with Laulima, State Parks, the District, and Placer
County related to shared parking in this area of KBSRA.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state
or local government agency.

Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are defined in Section 87103
as follows:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)) or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d).)

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).) An indirect interest in business or real property means a business
or real property interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of the official.

File No A-16-200
Page No. 5

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) "Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).)

• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $460 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.³ (Section 87103.)

The specific financial interests relevant to your inquiry are:

1. Business Entity: You have an indirect investment interest in LSC, the transportation consulting firm which your spouse is a ten percent owner of. (Section 87103(a).)

2. Sources of Income: LSC is a source of income. In addition, the clients of LSC are sources of income to you to the extent that their income to LSC is enough such that your spouse's pro rata share is $500 or more. (Section 87103(c).) Thus, Laulima, the corporation constructing a new North Tahoe Event Center is a source of income to you through LSC and therefore is also a financial interest of yours.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Section 87103 provides that a public official's interests are only disqualifying if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. (Regulation 1 8701.) Where the interest is explicitly involved, a financial effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. (Id.) The financial interest is explicitly involved when it is a named party in or the subject of the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) Neither LSC nor Laulima are explicitly involved under the regulation.

Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, the foreseeability standard is whether the financial effect can be recognized as a "realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 1 8701(b).)

Regulation 18702.1(b)⁴ sets forth the applicable materiality standard for a business interest. Regulation 18702.(b) states as follows:

³ Moreover, Regulation 18702 5 which provides that materiality standard for a disqualifying financial effect on an official's personal finances provides that if the governmental decision also affects a business entity or real property in which the official has a financial interest, the personal finances standard does not apply and materiality is determined under Regulation 18702.1 (business interest) or 18702.2 (real property). (Regulation 18702.5(c).) Thus, we do not further analyze possible effects on personal finances.

File No A-16-200
Page No. 6

"[T]he financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in . . . the value of a privately-held business entity."

Interests in LSC

LSC has been hired to work on the Crown Project. While in close proximity to the Event Center Project, the Crown Project is not directly connected to the Event Center Project. Moreover, you noted that the outcome of the Crown Project will not affect the compensation Mr. Shaw expects to receive for his professional services with Laulima. Based upon these facts, it does not appear that that there will be a reasonably foreseeable material effect on LSC from the KBSRA decisions.

Interest in Laulima

Generally, decisions regarding available parking at KBSRA could affect Laulima's Event Center Project as the Event Center is immediately adjacent to the KBSRA and relies on parking available at the KBSRA for people attending events at the facility. However, in this case, the use of the parking at the KBSRA is an ancillary issue to the Plan which focuses on rehabilitation of the existing pier and other facilities in the KBSRA. Moreover, the KBSRA is under the State's authority and you have provided no indication that the State has considered or will consider significant changes to the current parking at KBSRA or the Event Center use of the parking at the KBSRA. As you have indicated, the alternatives currently identified by the State range from reducing the number of available parking spaces by a mere 10 spaces to the addition of 22 spaces. Based upon these facts, it does not appear that there will be a foreseeable material effect on Laulima from the KBCRA decisions. Regulation 18702(b) provides that "the financial effect of a government decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant."

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

⁴ Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a source of income. Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is determined pursuant to the standard for materiality for the effect of a decision on an official's interest in a business entity contained in Regulation 18702.1.

his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state 
or local government agency.

• 
An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)) or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103( d).) 
• 
An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or 
more. (Section 87103(b).) An indirect interest in business or real property means a business 
or real property interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of the official. 


File No A-16-200  
• 
An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
''Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse 
and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official ( or his 
or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest. 
(Section 82030(a).) 
• 
An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $460 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate 
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.3 (Section 87103.) 
The specific financial interests relevant to your inquiry are: 
1. Business Emily: You have an indirect investment interest in LSC, the transportation 
consulting firm which your spouse is a ten percent owner of. (Section 87 l03(a).) 
2. Sources of Income: LSC is a source of income. In addition, the clients of LSC are sources

Where the financial interest is not explicitly involved in a decision, the foreseeability 
standard is whether the financial effect can be recognized as a "realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 18701(b).)

an official's personal finances provides that if the governmental decision also affects a business entity or real property 
in which the official has a financial interest, the personal finances standard does not apply and ma1eriality is determined 
under Regulation I 8702. l (business interest) or 18702.2 (real property). (Regulation 18702.S(c).) Thus, we do not 
further analyze possible effects on personal finances.

information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial
